humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLMs on multicultural knowledge across 45 countries, using cultural concepts (e.g., social etiquette, celebrations, religion, language) as a distinct evaluation domain.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only QA formatted as multiple-choice questions (CulturalBench-Easy) and binary True/False judgments for all options (CulturalBench-Hard).
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates factual recognition of regional customs, social etiquette, and language markers; assesses models' ability to recognize scenarios with multiple valid cultural answers (multi-mode behavior) rather than over-converging on a single mode.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is a domain-specific evaluation suite targeting global cultural and socio-behavioral knowledge.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 29 major models including OpenAI o1, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama-3.3, Gemma, Mistral, Qwen, and DeepSeek.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked for their cultural intelligence.

arts_humanities_data.note: The dataset spans cultural anthropology, regional studies, religion, and colloquial linguistics, mapping to Language and Linguistics, Religious Studies, History, and Performing Arts.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluated using standard accuracy metrics for multiple-choice and binary True/False tasks, comparing against human baselines.

published_after_march_2023.note: First published to arXiv in October 2024, with updates in 2025.

english_language.note: The paper and the primary evaluation benchmark are in English, with an auxiliary translation study included.

summary: This paper introduces CulturalBench, a text-based benchmark of 1,696 questions spanning 45 global regions and 17 cultural topics to evaluate the multicultural knowledge of LLMs. Developed through a human-AI collaborative red-teaming pipeline, the dataset includes multiple-choice (Easy) and multi-label binary True/False (Hard) evaluation formats to test model understanding of both single-answer and multi-answer cultural nuances.
key_findings: While frontier LLMs achieve near-human performance on the Easy multiple-choice version (up to 89.6%), they struggle significantly on the Hard binary format, with the best model (OpenAI o1) reaching only 61.5% compared to humans' 92.4%. Models exhibit substantial mode-seeking biases, failing heavily on multi-mode questions (average 20.9% accuracy compared to 49.6% for single-mode), and systematically underperform on questions related to less-represented regions like South America, Eastern Europe, and the Middle East.
review_notes: Ciop categorized humanities_role as 'both', while Cip chose 'domain'; resolved to 'domain' as the benchmark explicitly tests multicultural understanding as the target capability. Reconciled analytical_tasks to include both Ciop's domain-specific tasks and Cip's technical task formats. Kept Cip's addition of 'Visual Arts and Performing Arts' in ASJC areas to cover the entertainment and attire cultural topics.
